preFix.title: Setting storage heaters for Economy 7 tariffs
preFix.subtitle: Configuring input and output controls on modern and legacy storage heaters
preFix.excerpt: Storage heaters charge overnight on cheap off-peak electricity (Economy 7 or Economy 10), then release heat throughout the day. Incorrect input and output settings mean the heater either runs out of heat by early afternoon or overcharges every night regardless of the next day's weather. This explains the two controls and how to set them correctly.
preFix.sourceNotes: Energy Saving Trust storage heater guidance. Electricaire and Dimplex technical documentation (publicly available) on storage heater controls. UK Power Networks Economy 7 tariff guidance. Ofgem guidance on off-peak electricity tariffs.

content.text: A storage heater contains a core of high-density ceramic bricks with heating elements running through them. During the overnight off-peak period (typically 11pm-7am on Economy 7), the elements heat the bricks. During the day, heat from the bricks radiates into the room. An outer shell reduces convective heat loss; an output fan (on fan-assisted models) increases the rate of heat release on demand.

Two controls govern performance. The input control (sometimes called the charge control or night rate dial) determines how much charge to accept overnight. The output control determines how quickly heat releases during the day. Missettig either wastes electricity or leaves you cold.

On most heaters, the input dial runs from 1 (minimal charge) to 6 (maximum). A setting of 6 charges the bricks to full capacity every night. For an average UK winter day, a setting of 3-4 provides enough heat for a typical room without waste. The setting should track the expected next-day temperature: set higher the evening before a cold day, lower the evening before a mild day.
Cold day forecast (below 5°C): input setting 5-6. The full charge will be needed.
Mild day forecast (10-15°C): input setting 2-3. A partial charge is sufficient.
Warm day forecast (above 18°C): input setting 0 or minimum. No heating needed; accept only enough charge to maintain the chill-off setting.

On heaters without a fan, the output control opens or closes dampers that restrict the air pathway through the heater. Open damper (high output setting) releases heat faster. Closed damper (low output setting) retains heat longer, extending it through the evening. The common error: leaving the output control at maximum permanently. The bricks discharge all their stored heat by midday, leaving the room cold at 3pm.
Morning (7am-9am): output at medium (2-3 out of 6). Enough heat to warm the room quickly without depleting the core.
Day (9am-4pm): output at low (1-2). Slow background heat release while the room holds temperature.
Evening (4pm-10pm): output at medium to high, depending on how much charge remains and how cold the room is.

Modern storage heaters (post-2015) often include automatic charge control (ACC). This uses an indoor sensor and a schedule to set the overnight charge level for you. Set the target room temperature on the dial and the heater adjusts its input each night. Manual override is still available when the automatic setting gets it wrong.

Economy 7 off-peak hours vary by supplier and network: typically 11pm-7am or midnight-8am. Check your tariff documentation or call your supplier. The hours also shift by one hour on British Summer Time. Heaters with an internal clock may need updating in March and October. The off-peak period triggers the heating elements via a time switch in the electricity meter or consumer unit; the heater's input control governs how much of the available period is used.

attrs.fix: Reduce the output control to 1-2 during the morning. Increase it in the late afternoon to release the remaining charge into the evening.
attrs.cause: Output control is set too high, releasing all the stored heat in the first few hours
attrs.symptom: Heater is fully charged every morning but the room is too hot by midday and cold by evening

attrs.fix: Check the electricity meter time switch clock. Verify the off-peak hours with your supplier.
attrs.cause: The off-peak supply is not reaching the heater, either because the time switch has drifted after BST change or the tariff hours have changed
attrs.symptom: Heater does not feel warm in the morning despite input being set to maximum

attrs.fix: Check whether the heater has a direct-acting peak-rate boost element (common on combined storage/convector models). The boost should only be used as emergency heat; if it is running daily, the storage charge is insufficient for the room's heat demand.
attrs.cause: The heater is topping up with peak-rate electricity via a boost element
attrs.symptom: Electricity bills are higher than expected despite setting input conservatively

initialFindings.message: TECHNIQUE tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
brand-trademark: brand name "Target" — generic equivalent is "(any general-purpose shop)"
